old_claim.note: Human Anatomy & Physiology provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Darwin framed descent through mutual affinities, morphology, embryology, and rudimentary organs, making comparative anatomical evidence more specific than broad human anatomy.

source_shape.source_locator: 1859 first-edition text: the introduction names mutual affinities and embryological relations among the facts bearing on descent; chapter 13 covers mutual affinities, morphology, embryology, and rudimentary organs.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Darwin identifies comparative anatomical and developmental evidence as central facts supporting descent.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The comparative-anatomy node fits the morphology, affinities, embryology, and rudimentary-organ evidence; broad human anatomy is a less precise substrate.

ontology_before: Theory of Evolution (Darwin) depended directly on Human Anatomy & Physiology, implying human-body study was the relevant biological evidence substrate.
ontology_after: Theory of Evolution (Darwin) depends on Comparative Anatomy Collections, preserving the human-animal anatomical comparison evidence Darwin used while avoiding an overly broad human-anatomy edge.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: theory_of_evolution_darwin now depends on comparative_anatomy_collections and no longer directly depends on human_anatomy_physiology.
would_reject_if: The Darwin node is later narrowed to a purely botanical variation argument.
A more precise morphology_embryology_comparative_evidence node is added and replaces comparative_anatomy_collections.
The comparative_anatomy_collections node is narrowed to museum custody only rather than human-animal anatomical comparison.
short_reason: Darwin's morphology and affinities evidence is comparative anatomy, not broad human anatomy.
